questions.question: The power to enlarge the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court of India with respect to any matter included in the Union List of Legislative Powers rests with:
questions.option_4: The Union Ministry of Law, Justice and Company Affairs
questions.solution:
Jurisdiction of the Supreme Court

The Constitution of India provides a framework for the powers of the judiciary, but allows for legislative expansion under specific conditions.

  • Article 138(1): This article explicitly states that the Parliament may by law enlarge the jurisdiction and powers of the Supreme Court with respect to any of the matters in the Union List.
  • Article 138(2): Further allows the Parliament to enlarge the jurisdiction with respect to any matter as the Government of India and the Government of any State may by special agreement confer.
  • This ensures that while the core powers of the Court are constitutionally protected, the legislature can add to its responsibilities as the legal landscape evolves.

questions.question: The term of the Lok Sabha
questions.option_1: cannot be extended under any circumstances
questions.option_2: can be extended by six months at a time
questions.option_3: can be extended by one year at a time during the proclamation of emergency
questions.option_4: can be extended for two years at a time during proclamation of emergency
questions.solution:
Extension of the Term of Lok Sabha

While the normal term of the Lok Sabha is five years, the Constitution provides for exceptional circumstances under Article 83(2).

  • During a Proclamation of Emergency: The term of the Lok Sabha can be extended by Parliament by law for a period not exceeding one year at a time.
  • Limit of Extension: There is no limit on how many times it can be extended (one year at a time), but the extension cannot continue beyond a period of six months after the Emergency has ceased to operate.
  • Example: This provision was utilized during the National Emergency in 1975, when the term of the 5th Lok Sabha was extended twice.

questions.question: In what way does the Indian Parliament exercise control over the administration?
questions.option_2: Through Consultative Committees of various ministries
questions.option_3: By making the administrators send periodic reports
questions.option_4: By compelling the executive to issue writs
questions.solution:
Parliamentary Control over Administration

In a parliamentary democracy, the executive is accountable to the legislature. The Parliament exercises this control through various institutional mechanisms.

  • Parliamentary Committees: These are the primary tools for detailed scrutiny. Since the Parliament is a large body with limited time, committees like the Public Accounts Committee, Estimates Committee, and Departmental Standing Committees examine the administration's policies, expenditures, and performance in depth.
  • Consultative Committees: These are not parliamentary committees; they are informal forums attached to various ministries and presided over by the respective Ministers. They do not exercise formal 'control' in the same legislative sense.
  • Writs: Writs are issued by the Judiciary (Supreme Court/High Courts), not the Parliament, to control administrative actions.

questions.question: The Speaker can ask a member of the House to stop speaking and let another member speak. This phenomenon is known as
questions.solution:
Parliamentary Terminology: Yielding the Floor

Parliamentary proceedings follow specific protocols and terminology to maintain order and decorum during debates.

  • Yielding the Floor: This occurs when the Speaker requests a member who is currently speaking to stop and allow another member to address the House. It is a common technique to manage time and ensure diverse viewpoints.
  • Crossing the Floor: Refers to a member changing their party allegiance (switching from the opposition to the government side or vice versa).
  • Interpellation: The formal right of a Parliament to submit questions to the government. In India, this is essentially practiced during the Question Hour.
  • Decorum: The general requirement of maintaining proper behavior and dignity in the House.

questions.question: Which one of the following statements about a Money Bill is not correct?
questions.option_1: A Money Bill can be tabled in either House of Parliament
questions.option_2: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is the final authority to decide whether a Bill is a Money Bill or not
questions.option_3: The Rajya Sabha must return a Money Bill passed by the Lok Sabha and send it for consideration within 14 days
questions.option_4: The President cannot return a Money Bill to the Lok Sabha for reconsideration
questions.solution:
Characteristics of a Money Bill (Article 110)

Money Bills have a unique legislative track designed to prioritize the authority of the directly elected House (Lok Sabha) over financial matters.

  • Incorrect Statement (Option 1): A Money Bill cannot be tabled in either House; it can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha on the recommendation of the President.
  • Speaker's Authority: Under Article 110(3), the Speaker of the Lok Sabha has the final and binding power to certify whether a bill is a Money Bill.
  • The 14-Day Rule: The Rajya Sabha has no power to reject or amend a Money Bill; it must return the bill with or without recommendations within 14 days.
  • Presidential Assent: The President can either give assent or withhold it, but cannot return a Money Bill for reconsideration, as it is introduced only with their prior recommendation.
